intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: For a 'hate cooking' app, users may respond better to energetic, encouraging atmospheres that make the process feel less like a chore
facade.label: Bright and playful
facade.acceptImplies: Users want dopamine and motivation — the app should feel fun, rewarding, maybe even gamified or cheeky about shortcuts
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer stripped-down, no-nonsense interfaces that get them fed fast without entertainment or judgment

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a supportive, cheerleader-like tone that celebrates small wins and removes shame from kitchen failures
facade.label: Warm & encouraging
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into conversational, motivational voice with celebratory language; avoid clinical or detached instruction tone
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward neutral, straightforward, no-nonsense instructions; let the food speak louder than personality

evidence.hypothesis: For a 'hate cooking' app, users may respond better to energetic, encouraging atmospheres that make the process feel less like a chore
evidence.implication: Users want dopamine and motivation — the app should feel fun, rewarding, maybe even gamified or cheeky about shortcuts

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want personality and permission to be imperfect—casual handwritten type signals 'this app gets me, not some pristine algorithm'
facade.label: Messy handwriting font
facade.acceptImplies: App should feel approachable, human, forgiving of mistakes; playful tone in copy; maybe imperfect illustrations or rough textures
facade.rejectImplies: Users want clean, minimal, trustworthy-looking typography; avoid anything that reads as 'trying too hard' or cutesy; precision matters even if the task feels chaotic

facade.hypothesis: For a lazy cook, persistent access to key actions (search, filter, save) at thumb-reach is more valuable than maximizing recipe content space
facade.axisTargeted: Interaction model: floating/sticky persistent controls vs. integrated inline controls
facade.label: Sticky bottom bar
facade.acceptImplies: User values quick task switching and constant access to navigation over seeing more recipe content at once
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers traditional scrolling and integrated controls, willing to scroll to search or navigate away from current view

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer immediate gratification—gestures that execute instantly rather than reveal menus first
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior: instant response vs. layered navigation
facade.label: Instant swipe actions
facade.acceptImplies: Build quick-trigger micro-interactions (swipe to skip, tap to add, tap to timer)—reduce friction for impatient users
facade.rejectImplies: Users value explicit confirmation and review steps before actions execute—build deliberate, multi-step workflows with visible feedback

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking prefer stripped-down procedural text over ingredient lists and nutritional data
facade.axisTargeted: dense data vs key metrics — what information density level reduces friction for reluctant cooks
facade.label: Just the recipe steps
facade.acceptImplies: Strip recipe views to only active cooking instructions; hide prep lists, yields, cook times behind toggles or secondary screens
facade.rejectImplies: Reluctant cooks need context and structure (ingredients visible, timing clear, portion sizes front-and-center) or they'll make mistakes and blame the app

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants recipes filtered by effort level upfront vs. hidden complexity revealed only when needed
Whether user wants instant gratification with minimal loading vs. exploratory browsing with discovery animations

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants recipes filtered by effort level upfront vs. hidden complexity revealed only when needed
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant gratification with minimal loading vs. exploratory browsing with discovery animations
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking may respond better to stripped-down, friction-free interfaces that get them to recipes fast without motivational fluff or visual clutter
facade.axisTargeted: Organic vs Digital / Warm vs Cool
facade.label: Minimal & no-nonsense
facade.acceptImplies: The app should prioritize speed and clarity over encouragement—users want to feel in control, not coddled
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer atmospheres with personality and warmth even when hating cooking—stripped-down design feels cold or unhelpful for a frustration-prone user base

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers only the easiest, fastest meals with minimal ingredients and steps. Perfect for busy people who want to eat well without the cooking fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite
Meals so easy, you'll actually cook them
Filter by time
Today's easiest meals
Pasta aglio e olio
10 min
3 ingredients
Scrambled eggs on toast
5 min
4 ingredients
Caprese salad
2 min
4 ingredients
